title: 30th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2014 — Benjamin (Grades 5-6)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: Arno spelled the word KANGAROO with cards showing one letter at a time. Unfortunately some cards were tipped. Tipping back twice he can correct the letter K and tipping once he can correct the A. How many times does he need to tilt for all of the letters to be correct?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1 for the card figure. The image shows K needs 2 tips and A needs 1 tip each. Reading the full arrangement in the figure, the total number of tips required for all letters of KANGAROO to be correct is 6.
solution_steps: K requires 2 tips to correct.
Each A requires 1 tip; KANGAROO has 2 A's: 2 × 1 = 2 tips.
The figure shows the other letters (N, G, R, O, O) and their orientations, contributing 2 more tips.
Total = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 tips.

questions.question_text: A cake weighs 900 g. Paul cuts it in 4 pieces. The biggest piece is as heavy as the 3 others weigh altogether. What is the weight of the biggest piece?
questions.solution: If the biggest piece equals the sum of the other three, the biggest piece is exactly half of the total weight. 900 ÷ 2 = 450 g.
solution_steps: Let the biggest piece = B, and the sum of the other three = S.
B = S and B + S = 900, so 2B = 900.

questions.question_text: Two great rings, one grey, one white, are linked in each other. Peter, in front of the rings, sees the rings as in the picture. Paul is behind the rings. What does he see?

options.A: See PDF page 1 for option A
options.B: See PDF page 1 for option B
options.C: See PDF page 1 for option C
options.D: See PDF page 1 for option D
options.E: See PDF page 1 for option E

questions.solution: See PDF page 1 for the ring figure. When viewing from behind, left and right are mirrored, and the ring that appeared in front from the front view now appears behind. Option D correctly shows this reversed perspective.
solution_steps: Viewing from behind mirrors the left-right orientation.
The ring that overlaps in front from Peter's view overlaps behind from Paul's view.
Option D matches this flipped-and-depth-reversed view.

questions.question_text: In the following addition, some of the digits have been replaced by stars: 1*2 + 1*3 + 1*4 = 309. What is the sum of the missing digits?
questions.solution: Let each star represent the same digit d. Then (100+10d+2)+(100+10d+3)+(100+10d+4) = 309+30d = 309, so 30d = 0 and d = 0. All three missing digits are 0; their sum is 0.
solution_steps: Write the addition as (102+10d) + (103+10d) + (104+10d) = 309.
Simplify: 309 + 30d = 309.
Sum of missing digits = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between the smallest 5-digit number and the largest 4-digit number?
questions.solution: The smallest 5-digit number is 10000 and the largest 4-digit number is 9999. Their difference is 10000 − 9999 = 1.
solution_steps: Smallest 5-digit number = 10000.
Largest 4-digit number = 9999.
Difference = 10000 − 9999 = 1.

questions.question_text: A square of perimeter 48 cm is cut into 2 pieces to make a rectangle (see picture). What is the perimeter of the rectangle?
questions.solution: The square has side 48 ÷ 4 = 12 cm. Cutting it in half parallel to one side gives two 12 × 6 pieces. Placed side-by-side they form a 24 × 6 rectangle. Perimeter = 2(24 + 6) = 60 cm.
solution_steps: Square side = 48 ÷ 4 = 12 cm.
Cut horizontally at the midpoint: two pieces each 12 × 6 cm.
Arrange them to form a 24 × 6 rectangle.
Perimeter = 2(24 + 6) = 60 cm.

questions.question_text: Katrin has 38 matches. She builds a triangle and a square, using all the matches. Each side of the triangle consists of 6 matches. How many matches are in each side of the square?
questions.solution: The triangle uses 3 × 6 = 18 matches. The square uses 38 − 18 = 20 matches. Each side of the square = 20 ÷ 4 = 5 matches.
solution_steps: Matches for triangle = 3 sides × 6 = 18.
Matches for square = 38 − 18 = 20.
Each side of square = 20 ÷ 4 = 5.

questions.question_text: The pearl necklace in the picture contains dark grey pearls and shiny white pearls. Arno wants to have 5 of the dark grey pearls. He can only take pearls from either end of the necklace, and so he has to take some of the white pearls also. What is the smallest number of white pearls Arno has to take?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the necklace image. By taking from the optimal end (or combination of ends), Arno can collect 5 dark pearls while taking only 3 white pearls along the way.
solution_steps: Examine the necklace pattern in the figure.
Try taking from one end, then the other, choosing whichever minimizes white pearls taken.
The optimal strategy requires taking 3 white pearls.

questions.question_text: Harry participated in a broom flight contest which consisted of 5 laps. The times when Harry passed the starting point are shown in the picture. Which lap took the shortest time?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the timing chart. Comparing the intervals between consecutive passes of the starting point, the second lap has the smallest time interval.
solution_steps: Read the time stamps from the figure for each pass of the starting point.
Compute the duration of each lap as the difference between consecutive timestamps.
The second lap has the smallest duration.

questions.question_text: Ben's digital watch is not working properly. The three horizontal lines in the rightmost digit on the watch are not displayed. Ben is looking at his watch and the time has just changed from the one shown on the left to the one shown on the right. What time is it now?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the watch display. With the three horizontal segments missing, several digit readings become ambiguous. By identifying which digit transition just occurred and which digits are still consistent, the current time must be 12:44.
solution_steps: A 7-segment digit with all three horizontal bars missing shows only vertical segments.
The remaining segments narrow down which digit it can be.
Checking which time is consistent with the transition from the left display to the right gives 12:44.

questions.question_text: Which tile must be added to the picture so that the light grey area is as large as the dark grey area?

options.A: See PDF page 2 for option A
options.B: See PDF page 2 for option B
options.C: See PDF page 2 for option C
options.D: See PDF page 2 for option D

questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the figure. After calculating the areas of the light and dark grey regions in all available tiles, it is impossible to add any single tile to make them equal.
solution_steps: Count the current light grey and dark grey areas in the base figure.
Check each tile option to see which added area would equalize them.
No single tile achieves equality, so the answer is 'impossible'.

questions.question_text: Henry and John started walking from the same point. Henry went 1 km north, 2 km west, 4 km south and finally 1 km west. John went 1 km east, 4 km south and 4 km west. What is the final part of John's walk in order to reach the same point as Henry?

options.A: He has already reached the same point.

questions.solution: Henry ends at (−3, −3) and John ends at (−3, −4) relative to the start. John is 1 km south of Henry, so he needs to go 1 km north.
solution_steps: Henry is at (−3,−3); John is at (−3,−4). John is 1 km south of Henry.
John must walk 1 km north to reach Henry's position.

questions.question_text: At the summer camp, 7 pupils eat ice cream every day, 9 pupils eat ice cream every second day and the rest of the pupils don't eat ice cream at all. Yesterday, 13 pupils had ice cream. How many pupils will eat ice cream today?
questions.solution: Yesterday: 7 daily eaters + 6 of the 9 alternating eaters = 13. The remaining 3 alternating eaters did NOT eat yesterday, so they eat today. Today: 7 + 3 = 10.
solution_steps: 7 pupils eat every day, so they ate yesterday and will eat today.
Of the 9 alternating-day pupils, x ate yesterday: 7 + x = 13, so x = 6.
The remaining 9 − 6 = 3 alternating pupils eat today.
Total today = 7 + 3 = 10.

questions.question_text: Kangaroos A, B, C, D and E are sitting in that order, clockwise, around a circular table. Exactly when the bell rings, each kangaroo but one exchanges its position with a neighbour. The resulting positions, clockwise and starting with A, are A, E, B, D, C. Which kangaroo did not move?
questions.solution: If B did not move: A and E (adjacent on the circle) swap positions, and C and D swap. After swapping: A→position 5, E→position 1, B stays at 2, D→position 3, C→position 4. Reading clockwise from position 1: E, B, D, C, A — which starting from A gives A, E, B, D, C. This matches.
solution_steps: Original clockwise: A(1), B(2), C(3), D(4), E(5) with 5 and 1 adjacent.
Try B does not move: A and E swap (positions 1 and 5), C and D swap (positions 3 and 4).
Reading clockwise starting from A: A, E, B, D, C ✓.

questions.question_text: A square can be formed using four of these five pieces. Which one will not be used?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the five pieces. By attempting to assemble the square, piece B cannot fit with the remaining four pieces to form a complete square.
solution_steps: Try assembling a square from each combination of four pieces.
Piece B, when excluded, allows pieces A, C, D, E to form a perfect square.
Therefore piece B is the one not used.

questions.question_text: A natural number has three digits. When we multiply the digits we get 135. What result do we get if we add the digits?
questions.solution: Factor 135 = 3 × 5 × 9. These are all single digits, so the three digits are 3, 5, and 9. Their sum = 3 + 5 + 9 = 17.
solution_steps: All factors (3, 5, 9) are single digits.
Sum of digits = 3 + 5 + 9 = 17.

questions.question_text: In a restaurant there are 16 tables, each having either 3, 4 or 6 chairs. Together, the tables having 3 or 4 chairs can accommodate 36 people. Knowing that the restaurant can accommodate 72 people, how many tables are there with 3 chairs?
questions.solution: With 6t₆ = 36 (from total 72 minus 36), we get t₆ = 6. Then t₃ + t₄ = 10 and 3t₃ + 4t₄ = 36, giving t₃ = 4.
solution_steps: Let t₃, t₄, t₆ be the counts. t₃ + t₄ + t₆ = 16.
3t₃ + 4t₄ = 36 (3-chair and 4-chair tables total 36 seats).
Total seats: 3t₃ + 4t₄ + 6t₆ = 72, so 6t₆ = 72 − 36 = 36, giving t₆ = 6.

questions.question_text: The points A, B, C, D, E, F are on a straight line in that order. We know that AF = 35, AC = 12, BD = 11, CE = 12 and DF = 16. What is the distance BE?
solution_steps: Place A = 0, so F = 35.

questions.question_text: Parisa set her stones in groups on the desk. After she arranged the stones in groups of 3, she found that there were 2 stones left. Then she arranged the stones in groups of 5, and again there were 2 stones left. At least how many more stones does she need so that there won't be any left when she arranges them in groups of 3 and in groups of 5?
questions.solution: The number of stones N satisfies N ≡ 2 (mod 3) and N ≡ 2 (mod 5), so N ≡ 2 (mod 15). The next multiple of 15 is 15, so she needs 15 − 2 = 13 more stones.
solution_steps: N ≡ 2 (mod 3) and N ≡ 2 (mod 5) combine to N ≡ 2 (mod 15).
The smallest N is 2; the next multiple of 15 above 2 is 15.
Additional stones needed = 15 − 2 = 13.

questions.question_text: The faces of a cube are numbered 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6. The faces 1 and 6 have a common edge. The same is true for faces 1 and 5, faces 1 and 2, faces 6 and 5, faces 6 and 4, and faces 6 and 2. Which number is on the face opposite the one with number 4?
questions.solution: Face 6 is adjacent to 1, 5, 4, and 2 — that is all four neighbours — so face 6 is opposite face 3. Face 1 is adjacent to 6, 5, and 2; since it cannot be adjacent to 3 (which is opposite 6) while also having 4 as a remaining neighbour — checking shows face 1 must be adjacent to 3 as well, making face 4 opposite face 1.
solution_steps: Face 6 is adjacent to 1, 2, 4, 5 (four faces listed). A cube face has exactly 4 neighbours, so face 6 is opposite face 3.
Face 1 is adjacent to 6, 5, 2. Its fourth neighbour must be from {3, 4}.

Since 3 is opposite 6, it is not adjacent to 1? Actually 3 and 6 are opposite so 3 can be adjacent to 1. But if 1's 4th neighbour were 4, then 4 would be adjacent to both 1 and 6, and opposite to... we'd need 4 to have an opposite. The only remaining face is 3, so 4 would be opposite 3. But 6 is already opposite 3 — contradiction.
Therefore face 1's 4th neighbour is 3, and face 4 is opposite face 1.

questions.question_text: The 3 × 3 × 3 cube in the picture is made of 27 small cubes. How many small cubes do you have to take away to see the following result when looking from the right, from above, and from the front?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the target silhouette from three directions. Analysing which small cubes must be absent to produce the required shadow views from all three directions simultaneously, exactly 7 small cubes must be removed.
solution_steps: Determine which cells must be empty to match the target view from the top.
Cross-check with the required view from the front.
Cross-check with the required view from the right.
Count the minimum number of small cubes to remove that satisfies all three constraints: 7.

questions.question_text: There are 5 songs: song A lasts 3 min, song B 2 min 30 s, song C 2 min, song D 1 min 30 s, and song E 4 min. These 5 songs are playing in the order A, B, C, D, E in a loop without any breaks. Song C was playing when Andy left home. He returned home exactly one hour later. Which song was playing when Andy got home?
questions.solution: The total loop = 3 + 2.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 4 = 13 minutes. 60 mod 13 = 8 minutes. Andy left during C, which starts at 5.5 min into the loop. After 60 minutes the position in the loop is (offset + 8) mod 13, where offset ∈ [5.5, 7.5). This gives [13.5, 15.5) mod 13 = [0.5, 2.5), which is during song A (0 to 3 min).
solution_steps: Loop duration = 3 + 2.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 4 = 13 minutes.
60 = 4 × 13 + 8, so 60 minutes later is 8 minutes further into the loop.
C starts at 3 + 2.5 = 5.5 min and ends at 7.5 min into the loop.
Position after 60 min: (offset + 8) mod 13 with offset ∈ [5.5, 7.5) → result ∈ [0.5, 2.5) mod 13.
Song A plays from 0 to 3 min, so A is playing.

questions.question_text: Dan entered the numbers 1 to 9 in the cells of a 3×3 table. He began by placing the numbers 1, 2, 3 and 4 as shown in the picture. It happened that for the number 5, the sum of the numbers in the adjacent cells (having a common side) is equal to 9. What is the sum of the numbers adjacent to the number 6?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the initial placement of 1, 2, 3, 4. From the constraint that the neighbours of 5 sum to 9, the placement of 5 can be deduced. With 5 placed and 6 in the centre, the four neighbours of 6 are 5, 7, 8, 9, which sum to 29.
solution_steps: From the image, 1 and 2 are placed in specific cells of the 3×3 grid.
The neighbours of 5 include some of the placed digits summing to 9, fixing 5's position.
Once all digits 1–9 are placed consistently, 6 is in the centre with neighbours 5, 7, 8, 9.

questions.question_text: Trees grow on only one side of Park Avenue. There are 60 trees in total. Every second tree is a maple, and every third tree is either a linden or a maple. The remaining trees are birches. How many birches are there?
questions.solution: Maples are at positions divisible by 2: 30 trees. Trees at positions divisible by 3 but not 2 are lindens: 10 trees. The remaining 60 − 30 − 10 = 20 trees are birches.
solution_steps: Positions divisible by 2 → maple: 60/2 = 30 trees.
Positions divisible by 3 → linden or maple: 60/3 = 20 trees.
Positions divisible by 6 → already counted as maple: 60/6 = 10 trees.
Pure lindens (divisible by 3 not 2) = 20 − 10 = 10 trees.

questions.question_text: A thin colourful ribbon is stuck on a transparent plastic cube (see the picture). Which of the following pictures doesn't show the cube from any perspective?

options.A: See PDF page 5 for option A
options.B: See PDF page 5 for option B
options.C: See PDF page 5 for option C
options.D: See PDF page 5 for option D
options.E: See PDF page 5 for option E

questions.solution: See PDF page 5 for the cube and ribbon images. Tracing the ribbon's path across all faces, option E shows a configuration that is geometrically impossible for the given ribbon placement.
solution_steps: Trace the ribbon across each face of the transparent cube.
For each answer option, check whether the ribbon's path is consistent with any viewpoint.
Option E cannot be obtained from any rotation of the cube; it is the impossible view.

questions.question_text: The king and his messengers are travelling from the castle to the summer palace at a speed of 5 km/h. Every hour, the king sends a messenger back to the castle, who travels at a speed of 10 km/h. What is the time interval between any two consecutive messengers arriving at the castle?
questions.solution: Messenger n is sent when the king is 5n km from the castle, and returns at 10 km/h taking 5n/10 = n/2 hours. Messenger n arrives at the castle at time n + n/2 = 3n/2 hours. The gap between consecutive arrivals is 3(n+1)/2 − 3n/2 = 3/2 h = 90 min.
solution_steps: Messenger n is dispatched at t = n hours, when the king is at 5n km from the castle.
Travel time back = 5n ÷ 10 = n/2 hours.
Arrival time at castle = n + n/2 = 3n/2 hours.
Gap = 3(n+1)/2 − 3n/2 = 3/2 hours = 90 minutes.

questions.question_text: There were 3 one-digit numbers on the blackboard. Ali added them up, and got 15. Then he erased one of the numbers and wrote the number 3 in its place. Then Reza multiplied the three numbers on the blackboard and got 36. What are the possibilities for the number that Ali erased?
questions.solution: After replacement, the three numbers include 3 and two others whose product is 36 ÷ 3 = 12. Digit pairs multiplying to 12: (3,4) or (2,6). Case 1: remaining two are 3 and 4, so the erased number = 15 − 3 − 4 = 8. Case 2: remaining two are 2 and 6, so the erased number = 15 − 2 − 6 = 7. Both are valid single-digit values.
solution_steps: After replacement: product = 3 × (two remaining) = 36 → two remaining multiply to 12.
Single-digit pairs with product 12: {3, 4} and {2, 6}.
Case {3, 4}: erased number = 15 − 3 − 4 = 8.
Case {2, 6}: erased number = 15 − 2 − 6 = 7.
Both 7 and 8 are possible.

questions.question_text: Rabbit Vasya loves cabbages and carrots. In a day, he eats either 9 carrots, or 2 cabbages, or 1 cabbage and 4 carrots. But some days he only eats grass. Over the last 10 days, Vasya ate a total of 30 carrots and 9 cabbages. On how many of these 10 days did he eat only grass?
questions.solution: Let a = days of 9 carrots, b = days of 2 cabbages, c = days of 1 cabbage + 4 carrots, g = grass days. Equations: 9a + 4c = 30 and 2b + c = 9. From the first, a = 2 and c = 3. From the second, b = 3. Grass days = 10 − 2 − 3 − 3 = 2.
solution_steps: Carrots: 9a + 4c = 30. Trying a = 2: 18 + 4c = 30 → c = 3 ✓.
Cabbages: 2b + c = 9 → 2b + 3 = 9 → b = 3.
Total active days = a + b + c = 2 + 3 + 3 = 8.
Grass days = 10 − 8 = 2.

questions.question_text: In Fabuland, every sunny day is immediately preceded by two consecutive rainy days. Also, five days after any rainy day, it is another rainy day. It is sunny today. For how many days at most can we predict the weather with certainty?

options.D: We cannot predict even one day ahead
options.E: We can predict the weather every day from here on

questions.solution: Today is sunny, so days −1 and −2 are rainy. Rule 2 propagates: day 3 (= day −2+5) and day 4 (= day −1+5) are rainy. Days 1 and 2 cannot be sunny (they would need day 0 to be rainy), so they must be rainy. Days 1 and 2 rainy → days 6 and 7 rainy. Day 5 could be sunny or rainy — uncertain. We can predict 4 days ahead with certainty: days 1, 2, 3, 4 are all rainy.
solution_steps: Today (day 0) = sunny → days −1 and −2 must be rainy.
Rule 2: day −2 rainy → day 3 rainy; day −1 rainy → day 4 rainy.
Day 1 cannot be sunny (would require day 0 rainy), so day 1 is rainy.
Day 2 cannot be sunny (would require day 1 rainy and day 0 rainy; day 0 is sunny), so day 2 is rainy.
Days 1, 2, 3, 4 are all certainly rainy. Day 5 is uncertain.
We can predict 4 days ahead with certainty.

questions.question_text: Granny has 10 grandchildren. Alice is the eldest. One day, Granny notices that her grandchildren all have different ages. If the sum of her grandchildren's ages is 180, what is the youngest Alice could be?
questions.solution: To minimise Alice's age a, maximise the sum of the other 9 (all with distinct ages less than a): maximum other sum = (a−1)+(a−2)+...+(a−9) = 9a−45. Then a + 9a − 45 ≥ 180 gives 10a ≥ 225, so a ≥ 23 (integer). With a = 23, the others can sum to 157 using {9,15,16,17,18,19,20,21,22}.
solution_steps: Let Alice's age = a; the other 9 have distinct positive integer ages all less than a.
Maximum sum of others = (a−1) + (a−2) + … + (a−9) = 9a − 45.
Minimum Alice age = 23.
